World Business Briefing
EUROPE GERMANY: ECONOMY STALLS The German econ- omy stagnated in the second quarter in yet more proof that Europe has not been immune from the economic slowdown in the United States. The federal statistics office said gross domestic product in the second quarter had grown 0.6 percent from a year earlier, the lowest level in more than four years, from 1.4 percent in the first quarter. However, the office said, investment, particularly in construction, was stagnant and exports declined. Analysts said, however, that one positive number was the 0.9 percent increase in consumer spending.   (Reuters) BRITAIN: PROFIT UP AT ROLLS-ROYCE Helped by increased sales of commercial jet engines and spare parts, Rolls-Royce said its first-half profit rose more than fourfold. Net income increased to £85 million ($123 million), or 5.35 pence a share, compared with £18 million, or 1.15 pence a share, last year. Sales grew 15 percent, to £3 billion ($4.3 billion). Sales of commercial jet engines grew 40 percent, and the number of engines already on planes that require spare parts and servicing is on the rise, the company said. The No. 2 engine maker in the world, Rolls is winning market share at the expense of Pratt & Whitney, a unit of United Technologies, which is the industry's third largest. General Electric remains the market leader.   Suzanne Kapner (NYT) FINLAND: LAYOFFS AT SONERA Finland's largest mobile phone network, Sonera, plans to lay off about 1,000 people, or nearly 9 percent of its work force, to cut costs. The company said the moves would help reduce operating costs by about 60 million euros ($55 million), but no timetable was announced.   (AP) PORTUGAL: JOINT AUTO VENTURE Portugal's biggest oil and gas company, Galpenergia SGPS, and Midas Inc. are discussing a joint venture to open as many as 60 car parts and service stores.
